sourceMovie.title: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas (1998) stuck because it commits to its adventure premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Terry Gilliam's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The adventure and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Inherent Vice
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas clicked for you, Inherent Vice (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Easy Rider
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas clicked for you, Easy Rider (1969) is a strong next stop: both trade in adventure and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas?
faq.answer: Try Inherent Vice, Easy Rider, Withnail & I—each shares DNA with Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas?
faq.answer: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas (1998) is best described as Adventure, Drama, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this adventure lane.
